title: Neill Blomkamp’s new zombie AI ‘film’ is just slop warmed over
summary: On Monday, District 9 and Gran Turismo director Neill Blomkamp unveiled his latest project: a 13-minute sci-fi short titled Nightborne that's loosely based on Peter Watts' 2014 novel Echopraxia. The short comes from Blomkamp's new AI startup / production company, Barley Studios,…
whatHappening: Director Neill Blomkamp has unveiled a 13-minute sci-fi short called Nightborne created using AI technology through his new startup Barley Studios. The project is loosely based on Peter Watts' 2014 novel Echopraxia and has drawn criticism for being low-quality AI-generated content.
whoInvolved: Neill Blomkamp is known for directing acclaimed sci-fi films like District 9 and Gran Turismo. He recently launched Barley Studios, an AI startup and production company focused on creating film and video content.
whyMatters: This signals how established Hollywood directors are embracing AI tools to generate content at scale, potentially disrupting traditional filmmaking workflows. However, the critical reception suggests audiences and critics are skeptical of AI-generated storytelling quality, raising questions about whether AI-first production can compete with traditional filmmaking.
moreContext: The AI-generated content trend has accelerated significantly in entertainment, with various studios experimenting with generative tools for visual effects, scripts, and full productions. Blomkamp's project represents a major director betting on AI as a primary creative medium rather than a supporting tool. The critical label of 'slop' reflects growing industry concern that flooding markets with AI content prioritizes quantity over artistic merit. This comes amid ongoing debates about AI's role in creative industries and its impact on traditional filmmakers and workers.

title: The Light Flip is a minimalist flip phone with a point to prove
summary: It’s a Light Phone. It’s a flip phone. | Photo: Light A few years ago, the team at Light shipped the most smartphone-like device they'd ever created. The Light Phone III still kept to its minimalist, no-distractions ethos, but it was a phone-size rectangle with a phone-size…
whatHappening: Light is launching the Light Flip, a minimalist flip phone that combines the company's distraction-free philosophy with a nostalgic form factor. The device represents a departure from their recent rectangular smartphone designs while maintaining their core mission of reducing digital overwhelm.
whoInvolved: Light is a company founded on the principle of creating intentional, distraction-free devices that prioritize well-being over endless features. They've built a niche following among users seeking alternatives to mainstream smartphones.
whyMatters: The Light Flip signals growing consumer interest in alternative phone designs that reject constant connectivity and social media engagement. This reflects a broader cultural shift toward digital minimalism and could influence how other manufacturers think about feature-light device categories.
moreContext: Light previously released the Light Phone III, their most smartphone-like device to date, which featured a rectangular form factor but retained their minimalist ethos. The flip phone form factor itself has experienced a resurgence in recent years, with Samsung, Motorola, and others reintroducing foldable and flip designs. Light's version distinguishes itself by leaning into simplicity rather than technical innovation, targeting consumers fatigued by feature-bloated smartphones. The company has cultivated a dedicated community of users willing to pay premium prices for intentionally limited devices.

title: Here are the 30,000 songs Sony is suing Udio’s AI music generator over
summary: Sony Music Entertainment has filed another lawsuit against Udio, accusing the AI music generator of infringing the copyright of more than 30,000 of its songs, ranging from Elvis Presley's Hound Dog to Beyoncé's Say My Name, and Harry Styles' As It Was. The lawsuit, filed in a…
whatHappening: Sony Music Entertainment has filed a lawsuit against Udio, an AI music generator, alleging copyright infringement of over 30,000 of its songs. The lawsuit targets the platform's use of copyrighted tracks ranging from classic hits like Elvis Presley's "Hound Dog" to recent chart-toppers like Beyoncé's "Say My Name" and Harry Styles' "As It Was."
whoInvolved: Sony Music Entertainment, one of the three major record labels controlling roughly two-thirds of the global music market, is suing Udio, an AI music generation startup that allows users to create original songs using artificial intelligence. Udio competes with other AI music tools like OpenAI's Jukebox and Google's MusicLM.
whyMatters: This lawsuit represents a major escalation in the music industry's battle against AI training on copyrighted material without permission or compensation. The outcome could set legal precedent for how AI companies must handle music rights and potentially reshape the economics of AI music generation. A Sony victory could force Udio to license music or significantly limit its training data.
moreContext: Sony and other major labels have been aggressively pursuing legal action against AI music platforms over concerns that generative AI trained on their catalogs threatens artist revenue and intellectual property rights. Similar lawsuits have been filed against other AI platforms, and the music industry has expressed fears that AI-generated music could devalue human artists' work. This case follows broader tech industry battles over AI training data, with questions about whether AI training constitutes fair use under copyright law remaining largely unsettled in courts.

title: The FCC is planning to retroactively ban disguised DJI gadgets
summary: The Skyrover X1 vs the DJI Mini 4 Pro. | Image: AirPhotography Last October, we told you how the FCC had given itself the power to retroactively ban gadgets that have already received its approval to be imported and sold in the United States. Now, the FCC's getting ready to…
whatHappening: The FCC is preparing to retroactively ban DJI drones that were disguised or misrepresented during the approval process, even though they've already received clearance to be sold in the US. This marks the agency's first major enforcement action using powers it granted itself last October to revoke previously approved gadgets.
whoInvolved: The FCC (Federal Communications Commission) is the US regulatory body overseeing device approvals and spectrum use. DJI, the Chinese drone manufacturer and market leader, is the company whose products face potential retroactive bans for allegedly concealing their true identity during the approval process.
whyMatters: This sets a precedent for the FCC to police gadgets already in circulation and challenge Chinese tech companies' transparency in regulatory submissions. The action could prompt a broader review of approved devices and create uncertainty in the consumer drone market, potentially affecting existing DJI Mini 4 Pro owners and similar products.
moreContext: The FCC granted itself retroactive banning authority last October amid growing concerns about Chinese tech companies and national security. DJI has faced repeated scrutiny from US regulators and lawmakers over data collection and ties to the Chinese government. The company has previously faced import restrictions and export controls. This enforcement action suggests the FCC is willing to use its new powers aggressively to address what it views as deceptive approval practices.

title: The Odyssey turned me into an IMAX believer
summary: After seeing Christopher Nolan's The Odyssey for the first time early last week, I came away impressed, but somewhat conflicted about two of the film's more fantastical set pieces. While those scenes were beautifully crafted and tremendously acted, there was something a little…
whatHappening: A film critic at The Verge has become convinced of IMAX's value as a theatrical format after watching Christopher Nolan's The Odyssey in early screenings. The experience prompted a reconsideration of the technology's merits, particularly for large-scale cinematic set pieces.
whoInvolved: Christopher Nolan, the acclaimed filmmaker known for ambitious sci-fi and action films, directed The Odyssey. The Verge, a major technology and culture publication, published the review reflecting on the IMAX experience.
whyMatters: This endorsement signals IMAX's continued relevance in premium theatrical experiences at a time when streaming and home viewing options proliferate. Positive critical reception of IMAX presentations for major studio films can influence both filmmaker choices and consumer ticket-buying decisions for big-budget releases.
moreContext: Christopher Nolan has long been a champion of theatrical formats and large-format filmmaking, frequently shooting in IMAX and 70mm. The Odyssey represents a significant studio release, and critical commentary on its technical presentation carries weight in Hollywood's ongoing conversation about theatrical exhibition standards. IMAX has faced competition from other premium formats like Dolby Cinema, making positive coverage from influential tech-focused publications particularly noteworthy for the format's positioning.

title: Dr. Jill Lepore on why AI backlash is vital for the future
summary: Today, I’m talking with Harvard professor and New Yorker staff writer Dr. Jill Lepore about her new book, The Rise and Fall of the Artificial State, which comes out on August 25. Jill is one of the best writers there is at identifying institutional patterns in history, and…
whatHappening: Harvard professor and New Yorker writer Dr. Jill Lepore is releasing a new book, The Rise and Fall of the Artificial State, on August 25. She's making the case that public skepticism and backlash against AI is necessary and healthy for society's future.
whoInvolved: Dr. Jill Lepore is a renowned historian and staff writer at The New Yorker known for identifying institutional patterns throughout history. She holds a position at Harvard and has built a reputation as one of the most incisive writers analyzing technology and society.
whyMatters: The discussion around AI backlash is critical as the technology becomes increasingly integrated into institutions and daily life. Understanding the historical patterns and societal warnings Lepore outlines could shape how policymakers, tech companies, and the public approach AI development and regulation going forward.
moreContext: This conversation arrives amid growing public concern about AI's rapid deployment in workplaces, education, and government without adequate oversight or public debate. Lepore's expertise in institutional history positions her to draw parallels between AI's rise and previous technological transformations that disrupted society. The book's title suggests a cyclical pattern—that artificial systems, like other institutions, may experience periods of dominance followed by inevitable decline. Her work contributes to a broader cultural moment where voices are pushing back against techno-utopianism and demanding more thoughtful integration of AI into society.

title: Kodak EC35 is a dirt-cheap point-and-shoot film camera
summary: I’m partial to that avocado green one. | Image: Reto Project Following the success of its $99 Kodak-branded Snapic A1, Reto Project is releasing the Kodak EC35, an even more affordable 35mm film camera for just $34.99. The EC35 certainly isn't fancy. Its 25mm acrylic lens with a…
whatHappening: Reto Project is launching the Kodak EC35, a stripped-down 35mm film camera priced at just $34.99. The ultra-affordable point-and-shoot features a basic 25mm acrylic lens and minimal frills, positioning itself as an entry point to film photography.
whoInvolved: Reto Project, the company behind the release, previously found success with the $99 Kodak-branded Snapic A1. They're leveraging the Kodak name to bring budget-friendly film cameras to mainstream consumers.
whyMatters: The EC35 dramatically lowers the barrier to film photography, making it accessible to casual users and Gen Z audiences curious about analog cameras. This pricing strategy could spark renewed interest in film as a medium and expand Reto Project's market beyond enthusiasts to everyday consumers.
moreContext: Film photography has experienced a resurgence among younger generations seeking tangible, creative alternatives to digital photography. Reto Project's previous Snapic A1 demonstrated market appetite for affordable film cameras at under $100. The EC35's $34.99 price point undercuts that model significantly, suggesting the company is racing to capture price-sensitive consumers. This reflects broader industry trends of major brands—including Kodak—capitalizing on film's cultural comeback through accessible product lines.

title: I hate that I don’t hate this song made with Suno
summary: 1010Benja won’t apologize for using AI. | Image: 1010Benja / Instagram I would never go so far as to say there's no place for AI in music (I'm a fan of Holly Herndon, after all). But I generally find music made with generative AI to be offensively boring, especially the outputs…
whatHappening: A music creator named 1010Benja has released a song made with AI music generation tool Suno, sparking debate about the quality and legitimacy of AI-generated music. The artist is unapologetic about using the technology, even as critics dismiss AI music as creatively bankrupt.
whoInvolved: 1010Benja is a musician/creator who publicly embraced AI music generation. Suno is an AI music generation platform that allows users to create songs from text prompts, competing in a growing market of generative music tools.
whyMatters: The story reflects a cultural turning point where AI-generated music is becoming harder to ignore or dismiss outright, forcing the industry to reckon with authenticity questions. As these tools improve and more creators adopt them, the music industry faces disruption similar to what generative AI has triggered in visual arts and writing.
moreContext: Generative AI music tools like Suno have emerged in 2023-2024 as the technology races ahead of industry standards and artist protections. The debate mirrors broader tensions in creative fields: while some artists like Holly Herndon have experimentally integrated AI into legitimate work, most AI-generated music has been criticized as formulaic and creatively hollow. Copyright questions loom large as these tools are trained on existing music, raising concerns about artist compensation and consent.

title: This unpronounceable series of glyphs is an incredible side project from Kieran Hebden (aka Four Tet)
whatHappening: Kieran Hebden, the electronic musician behind Four Tet, has launched an unusual side project featuring an unpronounceable series of Unicode glyphs and corrupted characters as its name. The project apparently involves significant amounts of porcelain, though details remain deliberately obscure.
whoInvolved: Kieran Hebden is an acclaimed British electronic musician and producer known for his experimental Four Tet project, which has influenced ambient and electronic music since the late 1990s. The Verge is reporting on this quirky venture into his creative side explorations.
whyMatters: The project demonstrates how established artists continue to pursue unconventional creative outlets outside their primary work, blending digital aesthetics with physical materials. It reflects broader trends of artists using internet culture and glitch aesthetics as legitimate artistic expression.
moreContext: Four Tet has a long history of experimental output and surprise releases, often dropping music in unexpected ways on platforms like SoundCloud and Bandcamp. Hebden is known for his interest in both cutting-edge production techniques and analog materials, frequently incorporating field recordings and found sounds. The deliberate use of corrupted Unicode characters as a project name taps into glitch art and internet culture aesthetics that have gained mainstream recognition in digital art circles. This unnamed project continues Hebden's pattern of keeping audiences guessing about his creative intentions and outputs.

title: Orchid is a delightfully retro and approachable hipster synth
summary: A modern tribute to electric chord organs which were basically giant harmonicas connected to a fan. | Photo: Terrence O’Brien / The Verge In 2017, I bought an old Magnus chord organ off Craigslist for $10. It's one of my favorite music gear purchases. Electric chord organs let…
whatHappening: The Verge is reviewing Orchid, a modern synthesizer designed as a contemporary take on vintage electric chord organs. The instrument combines retro aesthetics with modern functionality, appealing to musicians and gear enthusiasts seeking approachable, characterful sound design.
whoInvolved: The Verge, a major technology and culture publication, is covering this synthesizer product. The review is written by someone deeply familiar with vintage music gear, having personal experience collecting and using classic instruments like Magnus chord organs.
whyMatters: This reflects a broader trend of synthesizer manufacturers creating instruments that balance nostalgia with modern usability, making vintage sounds accessible to contemporary producers. The retro-hipster appeal of chord organs suggests growing consumer interest in tactile, analog-style music production tools in an increasingly digital landscape.
moreContext: Electric chord organs were simple mechanical instruments popular in mid-20th century households—essentially keyboards connected to fans that produced harmonic textures. The reviewer's own $10 Craigslist purchase of a Magnus chord organ demonstrates the enduring appeal and affordability of vintage gear. Recent years have seen a resurgence in retro synth design, with manufacturers like Korg and Elektron creating modern instruments that echo classic designs. This mirrors broader cultural nostalgia trends and the revival of analog music production among younger musicians.

title: Google might not kneecap the Pixel 11a with an old processor
summary: The Pixel 10a shipped with a last gen processor. | Photo: Dominic Preston / The Verge Mystic Leaks suggests that the Pixel 11a will return to featuring a flagship-grade processor with the Tensor G6. Rather than the Tensor G5 found in the Pixel 10 and 10 Pro, the Pixel 10a…
whatHappening: Google appears poised to equip the Pixel 11a with a current-generation Tensor G6 processor instead of last year's chip. This marks a departure from the Pixel 10a strategy, which shipped with an older processor.
whoInvolved: Google manufactures the Pixel smartphone line and designs custom Tensor processors for its devices. Mystic Leaks, a reliable source for Android hardware information, reported the processor specification.
whyMatters: This upgrade could deliver better performance and longevity for budget-conscious Pixel buyers who typically accept compromises. It signals Google may be rethinking its practice of intentionally handicapping mid-range models to protect flagship sales.
moreContext: Google has historically used previous-generation processors in its "a" series phones—the Pixel 10a came with the Tensor G5 while the Pixel 10 and 10 Pro featured the newer Tensor G6. This tiered approach preserved pricing distinctions between product lines. If confirmed, the Pixel 11a using current-generation silicon would represent a reversal of that strategy and could indicate Google is prioritizing competitive performance across its entire Pixel portfolio rather than artificial segmentation.

title: Google is open-sourcing its 3D emoji
summary: Now, if you want to, you can use Google's 3D emoji in your own creations. The company shared some details about how it went about designing the little pictograms and why, as part of World Emoji Day on Friday. Things you might not necessarily worry about in a 2D illustration…
whatHappening: Google is open-sourcing its 3D emoji, making them available for use in external projects and creations. The tech giant shared design details and reasoning behind the emoji as part of World Emoji Day celebrations on Friday.
whoInvolved: Google, the search and tech giant known for its design work across digital products, is the primary player here. The announcement ties into the broader emoji community's World Emoji Day, an annual celebration of emoji culture and standardization.
whyMatters: Open-sourcing 3D emoji democratizes high-quality design assets, allowing developers and creators to build richer visual experiences without starting from scratch. This move could accelerate adoption of 3D emoji across apps and platforms, potentially influencing industry standards for how emoji are represented beyond 2D illustrations.
moreContext: Google's 3D emoji represent a evolution beyond traditional 2D emoji designs, addressing design complexities specific to three-dimensional rendering like depth, lighting, and perspective. The company's decision to share these assets reflects a broader trend of tech companies open-sourcing design systems and tools. World Emoji Day, celebrated annually on July 17, has become a platform for major tech companies to showcase emoji innovations and announce updates to their emoji libraries. This move positions Google as a contributor to the broader emoji ecosystem rather than keeping proprietary design advantage.

title: Dave Eggers told OpenAI staff  that ChatGPT was ‘silencing an entire generation’
summary: Dave Eggers attends the "The Turning Point: To Be Destroyed" premiere. | Image: John Lamparski/Getty Images for Tribeca Festival Last year, Sam Altman invited author Dave Eggers to give a talk to around 200 OpenAI staffers. The man has written countless novels, screenplays,…
whatHappening: Author Dave Eggers addressed roughly 200 OpenAI employees, warning that ChatGPT and similar AI tools are "silencing an entire generation" of creative writers and thinkers. The talk, arranged by OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, highlighted concerns about AI's impact on human creativity and intellectual output.
whoInvolved: Dave Eggers is a prolific novelist and screenwriter known for works like "A Heartbreaking Work of Staggering Genius." OpenAI, the company behind ChatGPT, is led by Sam Altman and has become central to the AI boom reshaping technology and creative industries.
whyMatters: The criticism from a respected literary figure amplifies growing concerns among creators about AI's threat to human authorship and originality. This signals potential tension between the tech industry and creative communities over who benefits from generative AI and what happens to traditional artistic careers.
moreContext: OpenAI's ChatGPT launched in late 2022 and has sparked widespread debate about AI's role in replacing human labor, particularly in writing, coding, and knowledge work. Authors and artists have raised concerns about AI training on copyrighted material without consent. Eggers' invitation to speak directly to OpenAI staff suggests the company is at least engaging with its critics, though his message indicates significant philosophical disagreements remain about AI's proper role in creative fields.

title: GoPro’s discounted Max 2 bundle includes $100 worth of accessories
summary: The GoPro Max2 can use your earbuds as a microphone over Bluetooth. A 360-degree camera is a great way to ensure you capture every bit of the action, but prices tend to be on the high end for models worth your attention. That’s why it’s notable that the GoPro Max 2 accessory…
whatHappening: GoPro is bundling its Max 2 360-degree camera with $100 worth of accessories at a discounted price point. The bundle aims to make the premium action camera more accessible by packaging it with essential add-ons.
whoInvolved: GoPro, the market leader in action cameras and wearable recording devices, is behind this promotional bundle. The company has built its reputation on rugged, compact cameras popular with adventure enthusiasts and content creators.
whyMatters: This bundle strategy lowers the barrier to entry for consumers interested in 360-degree video capture, a feature typically found on higher-priced models. The move could boost adoption of the Max 2 in a competitive action camera market while building customer loyalty through accessory inclusion.
moreContext: GoPro has positioned the Max 2 as a premium option in its lineup, and 360-degree cameras remain a niche but growing segment. The Max 2 notably integrates with standard earbuds via Bluetooth to function as a microphone, expanding its appeal beyond pure video capture. GoPro regularly uses bundle deals and accessory packages to compete on perceived value despite premium pricing. This strategy reflects broader industry trends where hardware makers bundle complementary products to justify higher price points and improve customer lifetime value.

title: Fine, electric mountain bikes don’t suck
summary: You don’t need a $10,000 Amflow PX Carbon Pro, but it doesn’t hurt. | Photo by Thomas Ricker / The Verge Cheater, I'd grumble between huffs as yet another e-bike rider casually skittered past me on a steep ascent. It's this purist attitude that, for years, has left me blind to…
whatHappening: The Verge publishes a reassessment of electric mountain bikes, challenging the long-held purist skepticism that e-bikes represent cheating in cycling. The article signals a cultural shift in how traditional cyclists view motorized trail riding.
whoInvolved: The Verge, a major tech and culture publication, is examining the e-bike market, which includes manufacturers like Amflow producing high-end models such as the PX Carbon Pro ($10,000+). The story centers on the evolving attitudes among mountain biking enthusiasts and the cycling community.
whyMatters: E-bikes are becoming mainstream in outdoor recreation, and legitimizing them in cycling communities could significantly expand market adoption and participation rates. This cultural acceptance matters because it removes social barriers to entry for riders of varying ages, fitness levels, and physical abilities, potentially democratizing trail access.
moreContext: Mountain biking has traditionally been dominated by purist attitudes favoring human-powered ascents, with e-bike riders often dismissed as taking an easy route. The e-bike market has exploded in recent years with increasingly sophisticated technology and premium offerings, forcing a reckoning with outdated gatekeeping attitudes. High-end electric mountain bikes now rival traditional bikes in engineering and performance, blurring the lines between legitimate riding and 'cheating.' This reassessment reflects broader tech industry trends of electrification and accessibility becoming normalized across multiple sports and outdoor activities.

title: Shark’s versatile ChillPill cooling system is back to its best price
summary: You can clip the versatile cooling gadget, wear it, and use it as a desk fan. | Image: Shark Portable fans are one of the easiest ways to stay cool during the summer, and you don’t have to spend much to find a decent one. If you’re looking for something more versatile, though,…
whatHappening: Shark's ChillPill cooling system is available at its best price point. The versatile gadget can be clipped to clothing, worn as a wearable device, or used as a standalone desk fan.
whoInvolved: Shark, the consumer electronics and home appliance company, has developed the ChillPill as part of its portable cooling product line. The device targets consumers seeking practical cooling solutions beyond traditional fans.
whyMatters: Affordable cooling options help consumers manage summer heat without major expenses or air conditioning reliance. A multi-use device like this provides value by replacing the need to purchase separate cooling gadgets, making personal temperature control more accessible.
moreContext: Portable fans have become increasingly popular as practical summer cooling solutions that don't require significant investment. The ChillPill represents an evolution beyond basic fan design, offering flexibility through multiple wearing and usage modes. This price reset comes as retailers adjust cooling product pricing throughout the season. Consumer interest in personal cooling devices has grown as people seek alternatives to traditional air conditioning systems.

title: Taylor Farms pulls iceberg lettuce from the US market after cyclosporiasis outbreak
summary: Agriculture - Harvested Iceberg lettuce / Santa Maria, California, USA. (Photo by: Tony Hertz /Design Pics Editorial/Universal Images Group via Getty Images) Food producer Taylor Farms released a statement on the Cyclospora outbreak Friday, confirming that it's "voluntarily…
whatHappening: Taylor Farms is voluntarily pulling iceberg lettuce from the US market following a cyclosporiasis outbreak linked to their products. The company issued a statement Friday confirming the recall as a precautionary measure.
whoInvolved: Taylor Farms is a major US agriculture producer based in Santa Maria, California, specializing in harvested produce. The company is one of the largest lettuce suppliers to American retailers and foodservice operations.
whyMatters: Cyclosporiasis is a parasitic infection that causes severe gastrointestinal illness, potentially affecting thousands of consumers who purchased affected lettuce. The recall impacts food supply chains and consumer confidence in fresh produce, while raising questions about agricultural safety protocols and oversight.
moreContext: Cyclospora outbreaks linked to fresh produce have occurred periodically in the US, with leafy greens—particularly lettuce and cilantro—identified as common sources. The parasite thrives in warm climates and contaminated water, making produce from certain regions more susceptible during specific seasons. Previous cyclosporiasis outbreaks have sickened hundreds and led to widespread recalls, prompting increased FDA scrutiny of produce suppliers. This incident underscores ongoing tensions between farm-to-table efficiency and food safety measures in an industry where contamination risks remain difficult to eliminate entirely.

title: Over half a million power tool batteries have been recalled due to a USB-C charging fire risk
summary: Greenworks Tools has issued a recall for around 554,780 Kobalt-branded power tools designed for yard work because of a battery issue "posing a risk of serious injury from fire hazard." There have been 34 reports of the tool's batteries "producing smoke, sparking or catching fire…
whatHappening: Greenworks Tools is recalling approximately 554,780 Kobalt-branded power tool batteries due to a USB-C charging defect that poses a fire hazard. The company has received 34 reports of batteries producing smoke, sparking, or catching fire during use or charging.
whoInvolved: Greenworks Tools, a major power tool manufacturer, issued the recall for its Kobalt-branded yard work equipment line. The affected batteries are USB-C rechargeable units used across multiple cordless power tool models in the Kobalt product portfolio.
whyMatters: The recall affects hundreds of thousands of consumer devices, creating immediate safety risks for homeowners and professionals using these tools. This incident highlights growing quality concerns around lithium-ion battery manufacturing and USB-C charging implementations in power tools, potentially triggering industry-wide scrutiny.
moreContext: USB-C adoption in power tools has accelerated as manufacturers seek standardized charging solutions, but implementation quality varies significantly. Battery fires in cordless power tools have become an increasing concern as lithium-ion density increases to improve performance. The 34 documented incidents represent a significant failure rate for a consumer safety issue. Greenworks Tools has instructed customers to stop using affected batteries and contact the company for replacement or refund options.

title: Samsung’s 55-inch Frame art TV is $200 cheaper than usual
summary: Samsung’s art TV looks right at home alongside real paintings. | Image: Samsung Samsung’s Frame is different from your average 4K TV. Its biggest selling point involves what it does when you aren’t actively using it. It can display art, turning your living room into a gallery.…
whatHappening: Samsung's 55-inch Frame art TV is currently discounted $200 below its typical price. The device functions as both a conventional 4K television and an art display when not actively watching content.
whoInvolved: Samsung, the South Korean electronics giant, created the Frame as part of its lifestyle TV lineup. The Verge, a major tech publication, is reporting on the pricing promotion.
whyMatters: The price reduction makes Samsung's premium art TV more accessible to consumers seeking stylish home entertainment alternatives. This discount could drive adoption of Samsung's gallery-focused TV concept among price-conscious buyers who value aesthetic home integration.
moreContext: The Frame TV addresses a growing consumer desire for multifunctional home devices that blend seamlessly with interior design rather than dominating living spaces. Samsung's strategy positions the Frame as furniture-like décor that doubles as entertainment, differentiating it from traditional TVs. The ability to display curated artwork during standby time appeals to design-conscious consumers and those in smaller living spaces. Promotional pricing on premium lifestyle products often signals inventory management or competitive positioning against similar offerings from LG and other manufacturers entering the art TV segment.

title: Netflix says around 300 titles used generative AI
summary: A scene from The American Experiment, one of the Netflix titles to use generative AI | Image: Netflix Netflix says roughly 300 titles on its platform used generative AI, most of which occurred in post-production. The streaming service revealed the news in its second-quarter…
whatHappening: Netflix has disclosed that approximately 300 titles on its platform have incorporated generative AI technology, with the majority of usage occurring during post-production phases. The streaming giant revealed this information in its second-quarter earnings report.
whoInvolved: Netflix, the world's largest streaming service with over 230 million subscribers, disclosed the AI usage data. The company has been increasingly exploring AI applications across content creation and production processes.
whyMatters: This revelation signals Netflix's significant investment in AI-assisted content production, potentially lowering production costs and accelerating time-to-market for titles. However, it raises questions about creative labor displacement and transparency with viewers regarding AI-generated content.
moreContext: Netflix has been experimenting with generative AI in various capacities, from thumbnail generation to script assistance. The disclosure comes as Hollywood grapples with AI's role in production, following 2023 writers' and actors' strikes that included concerns about artificial intelligence replacing creative workers. Most of Netflix's AI usage occurred in post-production tasks like visual effects, color grading, or background generation rather than primary creative elements, suggesting the company is positioning AI as a productivity tool for finishing work rather than core creative development.

title: Fortnite is getting a bunch of AI-powered ‘personas’
summary: Get ready for more AI characters in Fortnite. Developer Epic Games is going to let Fortnite creators publish experiences featuring characters with AI-powered voices starting on July 30th, and ahead of that launch, it's created 36 characters with "consistent voices and personas"…
whatHappening: Epic Games is rolling out AI-powered character personas for Fortnite, giving creators the ability to publish experiences with AI-voiced characters starting July 30th. The company has pre-built 36 characters with consistent voices and personalities for creators to use.
whoInvolved: Epic Games, the developer of Fortnite, is leading this initiative. Fortnite creators—the community members who build custom experiences within the game—will be the primary users of this new AI tool.
whyMatters: This move democratizes AI character creation for game developers and creators who may lack voice acting resources or technical expertise. It could accelerate the creation of diverse game content and experiences within Fortnite, potentially making the platform more attractive for user-generated experiences while raising questions about AI voice labor and authenticity.
moreContext: Epic Games has been investing heavily in creator tools and user-generated content features to keep Fortnite relevant beyond its battle royale core. The gaming industry is increasingly experimenting with AI for voice generation, character animation, and content creation. This announcement reflects broader trends of AI integration into creative platforms, where companies aim to lower barriers to entry for independent creators. Fortnite's creative mode already allows players to build custom maps and experiences, and AI personas represent the next evolution of those tools.

title: Apple’s OLED iPad Mini upgrade is on the way as prices continue to rise
summary: The current 7th-generation iPad Mini includes a Liquid Retina display. | Photo: David Pierce / The Verge The iPad Mini could get an OLED display upgrade as soon as October, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. It would be the most significant refresh for the Mini since its 2021…
whatHappening: Apple is planning to upgrade the iPad Mini with an OLED display as early as October, marking its most significant refresh since 2021. The move comes as Apple continues raising prices across its product lineup.
whoInvolved: Apple is the tech giant behind the iPad Mini refresh. Mark Gurman of Bloomberg broke the news about the upcoming upgrade timeline.
whyMatters: An OLED display would dramatically improve the iPad Mini's visual quality with better contrast, colors, and power efficiency compared to its current Liquid Retina LCD screen. This upgrade signals Apple's push to bring premium display technology across more product lines while facing consumer pushback over rising device costs.
moreContext: The current 7th-generation iPad Mini still uses a Liquid Retina display technology introduced years ago. Apple has already brought OLED to iPhones and larger iPad Pro models, making the Mini the logical next candidate for the premium display upgrade. The iPad Mini hasn't seen a major overhaul since 2021, making it due for significant improvements. However, the timing comes as Apple faces criticism for steadily increasing prices on nearly all its products, potentially putting pressure on consumers considering the upgrade.

title: Skullcandy’s bass-boosting Crusher headphones now come with Bose’s ANC
summary: Skullcandy announced a new version of its Crusher wireless headphones today featuring a few of Bose's audio technologies including its QuietControl ANC and head-tracking spatial audio. The Crusher headphone line differentiates itself from the competition through the use of both…
whatHappening: Skullcandy is launching an upgraded version of its Crusher wireless headphones that now includes Bose's QuietControl active noise cancellation and head-tracking spatial audio technology. This marks a significant integration of Bose's premium audio features into Skullcandy's consumer-focused product line.
whoInvolved: Skullcandy, known for its bass-heavy audio products and accessible price points, is partnering with Bose, a premium audio manufacturer renowned for industry-leading noise cancellation technology. The collaboration combines Skullcandy's signature sound profile with Bose's audio engineering expertise.
whyMatters: This partnership elevates Skullcandy's competitive positioning in the wireless headphone market by adding legitimacy through Bose's established ANC credentials, potentially attracting audiophiles to a more affordable brand. The move signals that even budget-conscious headphone makers are now competing on advanced features like spatial audio, which could accelerate consumer adoption of these technologies across price tiers.
moreContext: The original Crusher headphones built Skullcandy's reputation on customizable bass-boosting capabilities, differentiating them in a crowded market. Bose's QuietControl technology has long been considered the gold standard for active noise cancellation in consumer electronics. This collaboration suggests a trend of audio technology licensing and cross-brand partnerships as the wireless headphone market matures. The addition of spatial audio reflects broader industry movement toward immersive audio features becoming standard rather than premium offerings.

title: Apple’s reportedly raising the price for AppleCare Plus on Macs and iPads
summary: An AppleCare Plus subscription for a Mac or iPad will cost more soon, with prices going up by $0.50 per month or $5 per year for new sign-ups while remaining the same for existing subscribers, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. Gurman says that under the new AppleCare Plus…
whatHappening: Apple is raising AppleCare Plus subscription prices for Mac and iPad users. New customers will pay an extra $0.50 per month or $5 per year, while existing subscribers keep current rates.
whoInvolved: Apple is implementing the price increase, reported by Bloomberg's Mark Gurman, a reliable source on Apple's business decisions and product announcements. The change affects Mac and iPad owners considering or renewing AppleCare Plus coverage.
whyMatters: The hike represents Apple's continued monetization of its services business, which generates high-margin recurring revenue. For consumers, it modestly increases the cost of device protection, though the $0.50/month bump is relatively small and may push price-sensitive buyers to reconsider coverage.
moreContext: AppleCare Plus is Apple's extended warranty and support program that covers accidental damage, hardware failures, and technical support beyond the standard one-year warranty. The company has steadily expanded its services revenue stream, with AppleCare being a significant contributor to that growth. Previous price adjustments to AppleCare have typically occurred every few years as Apple adjusts for inflation and market conditions. This marks the latest in a series of incremental price increases across Apple's service offerings.

title: HP’s OLED-equipped 2-in-1 laptop is a solid back-to-school deal
summary: HP’s convertible OmniBook has a great feature set for the price. | Image: HP With storage and memory prices still at an all-time high, we’re happy to tell you about a solid deal on a good laptop when we find one, rare as they are. Best Buy is selling the HP OmniBook X Flip 2 in…
whatHappening: HP is offering its OmniBook X Flip 2 convertible laptop at a compelling price point through Best Buy. The OLED-equipped 2-in-1 device combines strong specs with affordability during back-to-school shopping season.
whoInvolved: HP, a major PC manufacturer, is promoting its OmniBook X Flip 2 through Best Buy, one of the largest consumer electronics retailers. Both companies are competing in the competitive convertible laptop market where features and pricing drive consumer decisions.
whyMatters: With memory and storage costs remaining elevated, affordable laptops with premium features like OLED displays are increasingly rare and valuable to budget-conscious buyers. This deal matters for students and professionals seeking performance and versatility without premium pricing during the critical back-to-school period.
moreContext: Convertible 2-in-1 laptops have become popular alternatives to traditional laptops and tablets, offering flexibility for different use cases. OLED displays, once exclusive to high-end devices, are becoming more common in mid-range laptops as manufacturing costs decline. The back-to-school season remains one of the most competitive shopping periods for tech retailers, with manufacturers and retailers using aggressive pricing to capture student and family purchases. Current market conditions show elevated component costs have made genuinely discounted quality laptops uncommon, making this HP deal noteworthy to deal-hunting consumers.

title: JBL’s Flip 7 speaker is small, versatile, and 40 percent off
summary: JBL’s Flip 7 is on sale in multiple colors. | Image: JBL Sure, Bluetooth speakers are awesome to have for picturesque beach trips, camping weekends, and other outdoor adventures. But they’re just as good to have for some sprinkler time in the yard with the kiddo, or for blasting…
whatHappening: JBL's Flip 7 Bluetooth speaker is currently on sale at a 40% discount across multiple color options. The portable speaker is being positioned as an affordable option for both outdoor adventures and casual home use.
whoInvolved: JBL, a subsidiary of Harman International (owned by Samsung), is a major player in portable audio equipment. The Verge, a leading technology publication, is covering the deal.
whyMatters: The sale makes an already competitively-priced portable speaker even more accessible to budget-conscious consumers looking for quality audio. This deal reflects ongoing competition in the portable speaker market where discounts are common tactics to drive sales during peak seasons.
moreContext: The Flip series has been a flagship line for JBL in the portable speaker category for years, known for combining durability, water resistance, and sound quality at mid-range price points. Bluetooth speakers have become essential outdoor gear, particularly as more consumers seek entertainment solutions beyond smartphones for travel and recreation. Sales promotions on established speaker models typically intensify during summer months when outdoor activities peak. The 40% discount represents a significant markdown, suggesting either end-of-season clearance or promotional competition from rivals like Ultimate Ears and Anker.

title: Starlink’s V5 dish is now available — here’s how it compares
summary: The smaller Starlink V5 (right) next to the larger V4 dish. | Image: SpaceX SpaceX's latest residential dish - the Starlink V5 - is now available in "select areas." It's notably smaller and lighter than the V4 dish with improved power efficiency. It'll be available in more…
whatHappening: SpaceX is rolling out its new Starlink V5 residential dish in select areas. The upgraded model is smaller, lighter, and more power-efficient than the previous V4 version.
whoInvolved: SpaceX, the aerospace company led by Elon Musk, continues to expand its Starlink satellite internet service. The company has been aggressively pushing satellite-based broadband as an alternative to traditional terrestrial internet providers.
whyMatters: A smaller, lighter dish with better power efficiency makes Starlink more practical for a wider range of installations and reduces operational costs. This incremental hardware improvement helps SpaceX remain competitive in the growing satellite internet market and could accelerate adoption in underserved areas.
moreContext: Starlink has been steadily rolling out hardware upgrades since launching commercial service in 2021. The V4 dish represented a previous generation of the technology that served as the baseline for residential deployments. The V5 represents SpaceX's continued iteration on the hardware design to improve performance and user experience. Broader rollout to more areas is planned, suggesting this is part of a phased release strategy rather than an immediate global availability.

title: Google and Epic give up fighting — third-party Android app stores are coming next week
summary: Epic Games and Google have just jointly withdrawn their attempt to retroactively settle the lawsuit that's changing how Android app stores work in the United States - and that means Google will be forced to carry rival app stores inside of its own. In fact, Google tells the…
whatHappening: Epic Games and Google have withdrawn their joint settlement attempt in their ongoing lawsuit, meaning Google will be forced to allow third-party app stores to operate within its own platform starting next week. This marks a significant shift in how Android apps can be distributed in the United States.
whoInvolved: Epic Games, the maker of Fortnite, has been fighting major tech companies over app store restrictions and commission fees. Google, which controls the Android operating system and Google Play Store, is the defendant in this case that will reshape mobile app distribution.
whyMatters: This decision opens Android to direct competition from alternative app stores, potentially lowering costs for app developers by reducing the commission fees they pay and giving consumers more choices for where they download apps. The outcome could set a precedent for how major tech platforms must operate their app ecosystems globally.
moreContext: Epic Games initiated legal battles against both Google and Apple over their app store monopolies and 30% commission rates. The lawsuit against Google has already resulted in court rulings requiring changes to how Android handles app distribution. This withdrawal of the settlement attempt means Google cannot negotiate its way out of these requirements, forcing them to implement sideloading and third-party app store options sooner rather than later. The case is part of a broader wave of regulatory and legal pressure on Big Tech's control over digital marketplaces.

title: Nothing’s good-looking Watch 3 Pro smartwatch is just $69
summary: The CMF by Nothing Watch 3 Pro comes in four different styles. | Image: Nothing While most fitness trackers are losing the screens to keep the price low, the CMF by Nothing Watch 3 Pro is a bit different. The budget-friendly smartwatch with a 1.43-inch OLED display is even…
whatHappening: CMF by Nothing is launching the Watch 3 Pro smartwatch at just $69, undercutting the market with a budget-friendly option that still includes a 1.43-inch OLED display. The device comes in four different style options, breaking the trend of fitness trackers ditching screens to hit lower price points.
whoInvolved: Nothing, the tech company known for affordable consumer electronics, is behind the CMF sub-brand that focuses on accessible pricing. The Verge, a major tech publication, is reporting on the launch.
whyMatters: This aggressive pricing pressures competitors to reconsider their value propositions in the smartwatch market, particularly affecting brands that have stripped features to compete on cost. The move signals that consumers can get OLED display technology with smartwatch functionality at a fraction of typical prices, potentially reshaping expectations for the budget wearables segment.
moreContext: The smartwatch market has seen a clear divide recently, with budget options typically sacrificing screens entirely to keep prices low, while premium models command $200-400 price tags. Nothing has built its brand around delivering tech features at aggressive price points, establishing itself as a disruptor in categories like smartphones and audio devices. The CMF sub-brand specifically targets cost-conscious buyers without compromising core functionality. This Watch 3 Pro continues that strategy by offering OLED technology—typically a premium feature—at an entry-level price point.

title: Sam Altman didn’t need another lawsuit
summary: OpenAI has spent the better part of the year involved in lawsuit after lawsuit, including one from the world's richest man. But last Friday, the company was hit with one of the highest-profile legal actions yet - from Apple. OpenAI's expensive hardware bet is what's on the line.…
whatHappening: Apple has filed a high-profile lawsuit against OpenAI, marking one of the most significant legal actions the company faces this year. The suit targets OpenAI's hardware strategy, adding to an already crowded docket of litigation the AI company is juggling.
whoInvolved: OpenAI, the AI company led by Sam Altman that's at the center of the AI boom, is facing legal action from Apple, the tech giant known for closely controlling its hardware and software ecosystem. OpenAI has been embroiled in multiple lawsuits throughout the year, including one from Elon Musk.
whyMatters: The lawsuit threatens OpenAI's hardware ambitions at a critical moment when the company is betting heavily on custom devices to compete in the AI market. A loss could force OpenAI to restructure its hardware strategy and drain resources already stretched thin by ongoing legal battles.
moreContext: OpenAI has faced an unusually litigious year with lawsuits from various parties, but Apple's involvement signals that even major tech partners view certain OpenAI initiatives as competitive threats. The hardware space has become increasingly central to AI companies' strategies as they seek to differentiate beyond software models. OpenAI's hardware push represents a significant capital commitment and strategic pivot away from its pure software focus. This lawsuit comes as tensions between major tech companies over AI development and device integration continue to escalate across the industry.
